title: The rate you really earn
deck: The advertised rate is not what we actually get. APY corrects for compounding frequency; the real return corrects for inflation. Two adjustments, one honest number.
objectives: Compute the effective annual yield (APY) and use CPI data to find the real (inflation-adjusted) return on a savings account.

content.text: Imagine two ads scrolling past on the same morning. Bank A offers a 4.85% annual percentage rate with daily compounding. Bank B offers a 4.95% rate with annual compounding. The eye drifts naturally to the larger headline number, 4.95%, and concludes that Bank B is the better deal. The honest comparison, once we run the formulas in this section, lands the other way around: Bank A actually pays slightly more.

Two effects make the advertised rate misleading. The first is the compounding frequency: the same APR pays more if compounded more often, as we saw in Section 3.3. The second is inflation: even an account that pays a real positive interest rate can be losing purchasing power if prices are rising faster. This section introduces the two standard corrections, annual percentage yield (APY) for compounding and real return for inflation, and uses them to read banking advertisements the way a finance professional would.

The annual percentage yield (APY) of an account is the single annual interest rate that would produce the same future value, after one year, as the actual compounding schedule does. It rolls every compounding decision the bank made into one honest number, which is the right tool for comparing accounts at different banks.
The formula falls out of Section 3.3 with one substitution: start the year with $1, run the compound-interest formula for t = 1, and read off how much it grew.

content.caption: Figure 3.4. The same advertised 5% rate produces three different answers depending on the question. APY adjusts upward for compounding; real return adjusts downward for inflation.

content.text:
Definition 3.4. The annual percentage yield (APY) of an account with nominal rate r compounded n times per year is the rate that, applied once over one year, gives the same growth:
APY = (1 + r/n)^n − 1
For continuous compounding, the limit is APY = er − 1.

Margin noteThe 1991 Truth in Savings Act requires US banks to advertise APY (not just APR) on deposit accounts, so consumers can compare offers fairly. Loan products such as credit cards and mortgages are still allowed to advertise APR, which is why credit-card statements always seem to charge more interest than the headline suggests.

An interest rate measures how many more dollars we have at the end of the year, but it does not measure how many more things those dollars can buy. If prices are rising at 3% per year and our account is paying 5%, our purchasing power has grown by only about 2%, not the full 5%. The real return is the rate we earn after subtracting the inflation rate.
APY tells us how many more dollars. Real return tells us how many more groceries.

Common mistakeComparing APR at one bank to APY at another. The two numbers measure different things; comparing them directly always favors whichever bank quoted APY. Always read the small print and convert to the same metric (usually APY) before deciding.

Example 3.4. Two banks both advertise “ 5%.”

Bank A advertises 4.85% APR, compounded daily. Bank B advertises 4.95% APR, compounded annually. Inflation is running at 3.0%. We want the APY at each bank, and the real return that the customer actually earns at Bank A.

Solution.

Step 1. Compute Bank A's APY. Daily compounding gives n = 365:

APY_A = (1 + 0.0485/365)365 − 1 ≈ 0.04969

So Bank A's APY is about 4.97%.

Step 2. Compute Bank B's APY. Annual compounding (n = 1) makes APY equal to APR:

APY_B = (1 + 0.0495/1)^1 − 1 = 0.0495

So Bank B's APY is exactly 4.95%.

Step 3. Compare. 4.97% (Bank A) beats 4.95% (Bank B), in spite of Bank B's higher headline rate. The compounding frequency closed the gap and then crossed it.

Step 4. Compute Bank A's real return. Subtract inflation:

real return ≈ 4.97\% − 3.0\% = 1.97\%

The customer at Bank A actually gains about 2% of real purchasing power per year, not the 5% suggested by the advertisement. The same exercise would be the right way to read any savings ad we ever see.

In the wildNewspaper articles about “ inflation-adjusted returns” on Social Security, retirement accounts, and bond yields are doing exactly the real-return subtraction in this section. When the Federal Reserve says it is raising rates “ to fight inflation,” the implicit goal is to drag real returns into positive territory across the economy. The same arithmetic that compares two bank ads also drives macroeconomic policy.

1. Find the APY for an account with 6% APR compounded monthly. Round to two decimal places.

    Show answer

    Answer. About 6.17%. APY = (1 + 0.06/12)12 − 1 = (1.005)12 − 1 &approx 0.0617.

  2. Find the APY for an account with 5% APR compounded continuously. Round to two decimal places.

    Show answer

    Answer. About 5.13%. APY = e0.05 − 1 &approx 0.0513.

  3. An account pays an APY of 4.5% in a year when inflation is 6%. What is the real return?

    Show answer

    Answer. About − 1.5%. Real return ≈ 4.5% − 6% = − 1.5%. The account loses purchasing power over the year even though the dollar balance went up.

  4. Bank A advertises 4% APR compounded daily. Bank B advertises 4.10% APR compounded annually. Which bank's APY is higher?

    1. Bank A
    2. Bank B
    3. They are equal.
    4. Cannot tell without the principal.
    Show answer

    Answer. B. Bank A's APY is about (1 + 0.04/365)365 − 1 ≈ 4.08%, while Bank B's APY is exactly 4.10%. The compounding bump at Bank A is smaller than the 0.10 percentage-point headline gap at Bank B.

  5. Which of these does APY not account for?

    1. The compounding frequency
    2. Interest earning interest within the year
    3. Inflation
    4. The actual one-year growth of $1
    Show answer

    Answer. C. APY captures compounding but not inflation. The inflation adjustment is the separate “ real return” calculation.

  6. If an account compounds annually, what is the relationship between its APR and its APY?

    1. APY is always larger than APR.
    2. APY equals APR.
    3. APY is always smaller than APR.
    4. APY depends on the principal.
    Show answer

    Answer. B. With n = 1, the APY formula reduces to APY = (1 + r)1 − 1 = r. Compounding more often than annually is what makes APY exceed APR.

So far every example has been one deposit. Section 3.5 introduces the more common situation in retirement planning: a steady stream of deposits, made on a regular schedule for years on end. The lump-sum formula generalizes into an annuity formula, and the difference between the two is exactly the difference between “ I have $1,000 to put away today” and “ I can put $200 away every month for thirty years.”
